title: A French Micro-Entrepreneur's Guide to Invoicing a US Client

author.name: Maria Kowalski
author.bio: Based in Berlin, Maria helps non-EU freelancers navigate the complexities of the European market. She's an expert on VAT, EU-specific invoicing requirements, and business registration across different EU countries.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Proactively provide a completed W-8BEN form with your first invoice to prevent US tax withholding and eliminate a common payment delay.
Offer local US banking details via a multi-currency account, allowing clients to pay with a simple ACH transfer instead of a costly international wire.
Declare revenue based on the exact euro amount that lands in your bank account, using the official ECB exchange rate for that day to create an audit-proof record.
Ensure your invoice includes the mandatory French clause 'TVA non applicable, art. 293 B du CGI' to legally justify your VAT exemption status.
Mirror your signed contract or SOW exactly in your invoice's line items and descriptions to prevent scrutiny and give the client's finance team an easy approval.
summary: For French freelancers, flawed invoices sent to US clients can cause payment delays and damage their professional reputation. The solution is to treat invoicing as a strategic tool by mastering a three-pillar framework: proactively manage US client compliance with forms like the W-8BEN, fortify the invoice with non-negotiable French legal mentions, and perfect cash flow using multi-currency accounts. By implementing this blueprint, freelancers can eliminate friction, ensure prompt payment, and build the trust that solidifies their status as elite international partners.
ai_overview_answer:
To correctly invoice a US client, a French freelancer must combine client-facing professionalism with strict French legal compliance and a strategic payment workflow. Proactively provide a completed W-8BEN form with your first invoice to certify your non-U.S. status and prevent tax withholding delays. Your document must be titled "Facture" and include all mandatory French details, such as your SIRET number, "EI" status, and the VAT exemption clause "TVA non applicable, art. 293 B du CGI." Ensure all line items perfectly mirror the signed contract to avoid payment disputes.

To facilitate prompt payment, invoice in USD but provide local US ACH banking details through a multi-currency account, making it a simple domestic transfer for your client. For your own accounting, you must declare the exact euro amount that lands in your bank account on the day it is received. Use the official European Central Bank exchange rate for that specific day to create an audit-proof record of the conversion. Finally, include legally mandated French late payment penalties, such as the €40 fixed recovery fee, to protect your cash flow.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

author.name: Maria Kowalski
author.description: Based in Berlin, Maria helps non-EU freelancers navigate the complexities of the European market. She's an expert on VAT, EU-specific invoicing requirements, and business registration across different EU countries.

step.name: Project Unquestionable Professionalism
step.text: Master client-side compliance to signal you are an experienced and low-risk global partner. Proactively provide a completed W-8BEN form to prevent payment friction. Ensure your invoice perfectly mirrors the signed contract or Statement of Work (SOW) to avoid scrutiny. Structure your payment terms for promptness, including legally mandated French late payment penalties.

step.name: Fortify Your French Compliance
step.text: Ensure your invoice is an ironclad legal document that mitigates your own regulatory risk. Include all mandatory legal mentions (*mentions obligatoires*), such as the word 'Facture', a unique sequential number, your SIRET number, and your 'EI' status. Correctly state your VAT exemption with the mandatory phrase 'TVA non applicable, art. 293 B du CGI' and consider adding 'Exonération de TVA - Article 44 de la directive 2006/112/CE' for international transactions.

step.name: Perfect Your Cash Flow
step.text: Implement a strategic workflow for receiving and converting funds that protects your earnings. Offer local US banking details via a multi-currency account to allow clients to pay with a simple ACH transfer. Invoice in USD as a courtesy, but declare revenue based on the exact euro amount that lands in your bank account on the day it arrives, using the official ECB exchange rate for that day to create an audit-proof record.

mainEntity.name: Do I absolutely need a W-8BEN form for a US client?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. Providing this form proactively is a mark of professionalism. It certifies your non-U.S. status, allowing your client to pay you without withholding US taxes and removing a common cause of payment delays.

mainEntity.name: How do I calculate the EUR amount to declare from a USD invoice?

acceptedAnswer.text: You declare the exact euro amount that arrives in your bank account on the day it is credited. This is the "cash receipt principle" (*le principe de l'encaissement*). Reconcile your records using the official ECB exchange rate for that specific day.

mainEntity.name: What is the best way to receive USD payments in France?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use a multi-currency account to provide your client with local US ACH banking details. This makes paying you as simple and cheap as a domestic transfer for them, removing the friction of international wires.

mainEntity.name: Can I invoice a US client in dollars?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, and you should. Invoicing in your client's currency is a professional courtesy. However, all your official accounting and social security declarations in France must be recorded in euros.

mainEntity.name: What if I make a mistake on a sent invoice?

acceptedAnswer.text: You cannot simply delete it, as this breaks the mandatory sequential numbering of your invoices. The correct procedure is to issue a new, corrected invoice (*facture rectificative*) with a new number that clearly references the number of the original, now-voided invoice.

body.content: For the French professional operating on a global scale, an invoice sent to a US client is far more than a request for payment. It is the final handshake of an engagement, a document that speaks volumes about your reliability, attention to detail, and respect for your client's internal processes. A flawed invoice creates friction, delays payment, and undermines your premium positioning. A perfect one builds trust, encourages repeat business, and solidifies your status as an indispensable international partner.

This is not about simply checking boxes for compliance. It is about architecting a seamless financial experience for your client that anticipates their needs and eliminates administrative drag. This guide presents a three-pillar framework to transform your invoicing from a mundane task into a strategic asset.

items: **Pillar 1: Project Unquestionable Professionalism.** Master the client-side compliance that signals you are an experienced and low-risk global partner.
**Pillar 2: Fortify Your French Compliance.** Ensure your invoice is an ironclad legal document that mitigates your own regulatory risk.
**Pillar 3: Perfect Your Cash Flow.** Implement a strategic workflow for receiving and converting funds that protects your earnings.

body.content: By mastering these pillars, you replace compliance anxiety with the quiet confidence of a true "Business-of-One."
Pillar 1: Project Unquestionable Professionalism

Your professionalism is judged long before you deliver the final product; it is demonstrated through the administrative rigor that signals you are a reliable international partner. This is about engineering a smooth, frictionless process for your client that prevents the very anxieties that bog them down in administrative tasks.
Proactively Eliminate Payment Friction with the W-8BEN Form

The single most powerful move a French freelancer can make when engaging a US client is to proactively provide a completed **W-8BEN form** with their first invoice. Do not wait for their Accounts Payable department to halt payment and request it. Sending it upfront is a clear signal that you are a seasoned global professional who understands their compliance needs.

The W-8BEN, or "Certificate of Foreign Status of Beneficial Owner," is an IRS document you provide to your client—not the IRS. Its purpose is twofold: to certify that you are a non-U.S. person and to claim benefits under the France-U.S. tax treaty to avoid double taxation. For your client, this form is critical. It justifies why they aren't withholding 30% of your payment for US taxes, protecting them from their own IRS compliance issues. By providing it before they ask, you eliminate a common bottleneck, prevent weeks of potential delay, and immediately position yourself as a low-risk, high-value partner.
The "Mirror Match" Principle: Align Your Invoice with Your Contract

Payment disputes often stem from a simple failure: the invoice does not perfectly mirror the signed contract or Statement of Work (SOW). Any discrepancy in line items, rates, or service descriptions is an invitation for scrutiny. The person approving your payment likely has no context for your work; their sole function is to match the invoice against the approved SOW. If the details do not align, their default action is to place it in a "pending" queue for clarification. This is where payments stall. Ensure every detail on your invoice is a direct reflection of what was contractually agreed upon to give their finance team an easy, undeniable "yes."
Structure Your Payment Terms for Promptness and Protection

Your payment terms are not a suggestion; they are a clear expectation you set for the business relationship. While "Net 30" is common, it is often not the most strategic for a freelancer. Consider stronger terms:

items: **"Payment due upon receipt"**: This is perfectly acceptable and encourages immediate action.
**"Net 15"**: A shorter timeframe that still provides a reasonable grace period.

body.content: Crucially, you must also include legally mandated French late payment penalties. This is not an aggressive tactic; it is a standard and required part of French B2B commerce. Your invoice must state:

items: **The late payment penalty rate.** A common and legally sound practice is to set your rate at three times the legal interest rate ("*trois fois le taux d'intérêt légal*"). If you don't specify one, a default rate applies.
**A fixed recovery fee of €40.** French law mandates that a €40 fixed indemnity for collection costs is automatically due the day after the payment due date.

body.content: Including these terms on your invoice educates your US client on standard European practices and protects your cash flow by codifying the consequences of late payment from the outset.
Pillar 2: Fortify Your French Compliance

While clear payment terms protect your cash flow, the foundation of a defensible invoice rests on meeting every strict, non-negotiable French legal requirement. Overlooking these details is not just unprofessional; it can invalidate the invoice, invite payment delays, and expose you to financial penalties. For a global professional, impeccable domestic compliance is the ultimate form of risk mitigation.
The Bulletproof Invoice Checklist

Before sending an invoice, verify that it contains every mandatory legal mention (*mentions obligatoires*). Think of this as a pilot's pre-flight checklist; skipping a single step can ground the entire operation. A missing detail gives a client's finance department a legitimate reason to reject the invoice.
Use this table as your final check:

header: Why It Matters (The Risk You Mitigate)
rows:
The word "**Facture**" (not "Invoice")
A **unique, sequential number**
The **date of issue** (*date d'émission*)
An incorrect title or a non-sequential number is a major red flag for tax auditors and can invalidate your accounting records.

Your **Full Name** followed by "**EI**" or "**Entrepreneur Individuel**"
Your **business address**
Your **SIRET number**
This legally identifies your business as a distinct entity. The "EI" mention is a legal requirement since May 2022 and signals your compliance with current French law.
Prevents ambiguity about who is being billed, which is critical if payment becomes overdue and requires formal follow-up.

A **clear description** of services rendered
The **date of service completion** (*date de la prestation*)
The **total amount due** (HT - *Hors Taxes*)
Vague descriptions are a leading cause of payment disputes. Details must be specific enough for an accounting professional with zero context of your work to approve it.

body.content: Missing any of these elements can result in fines starting at €15 per missing mention.
Mastering the VAT Exemption: The Two Tiers of Compliance
Simply stating that you do not charge VAT is insufficient. You must demonstrate *why* with precision, showing your US client that you are in complete control of your fiscal obligations.

items: **Tier 1 (Mandatory Baseline):** As a micro-entrepreneur under the VAT threshold, your invoice must legally include the phrase: **"TVA non applicable, art. 293 B du CGI"**. This non-negotiable text references the specific article in the French General Tax Code (*Code Général des Impôts*) that grants your exemption.

**Tier 2 (Expert-Level Signal):** To project a higher level of international expertise, also include a mention that speaks directly to the nature of the transaction. For B2B services provided to a client outside the European Union, the place of taxation is the client's location. Adding the phrase **"Exonération de TVA - Article 44 de la directive 2006/112/CE"** demonstrates a sophisticated understanding of cross-border VAT rules. While the first mention is about your *status*, this one is about the *transaction's* nature. It’s a subtle but powerful signal to a US client’s finance team that you are a seasoned expert in France-US commerce.

body.content: Pillar 3: Perfect Your Cash Flow

With your invoice now a masterclass in compliance, the final hurdle is ensuring the money travels from your client's US bank account to yours with minimal friction and maximum value. This is about architecting a payment workflow that protects your earnings and satisfies French regulators.
Eliminate Client Friction with a Superior Payment Method

The way you ask to be paid sends a powerful signal. Requesting an international wire transfer may seem standard, but from your US client's perspective, it is an expensive, manual process fraught with potential for error. It creates friction, and friction causes delays.

The expert move is to eliminate their burden entirely by providing local US banking details through a modern multi-currency account. This allows your client to pay you via an ACH (Automated Clearing House) transfer, which for them is a simple domestic transaction.

rows: Costly (often $25-$50), manual, requires extra banking details.
May incur receiving fees from intermediary banks.
Low-cost or free, simple, treated as a domestic payment.
Funds arrive cleanly without unexpected deductions.

body.content: By offering ACH details, you make paying you as simple as paying a local vendor. This removes operational friction and accelerates your payment timeline.
Invoice in USD, Declare in EUR: The Rules of Conversion

As a French micro-entrepreneur, your accounting and declarations to URSSAF must be in euros. While you should invoice your client in their native currency (USD) as a professional courtesy, the amount you officially record is governed by a core principle of French accounting: ***le principe de l'encaissement*** **(the cash receipt principle)**.

This means you declare **the exact euro amount that lands in your bank account on the day it arrives**, not the theoretical euro equivalent of the USD amount on your invoice. If you invoice for $1,000 and receive €930 after conversion, you declare €930 to URSSAF. This non-negotiable rule keeps your revenue declarations perfectly aligned with your actual cash flow.
The Audit-Proof Conversion Rate

When recording your revenue in euros, using an arbitrary rate from a Google search is a significant compliance risk. The French tax administration expects you to use a verifiable, official exchange rate for the day the funds were received.

To make your accounting unassailable, use the official daily exchange rate published by a major central bank for the **exact date the funds were credited to your account**. The most authoritative sources are:

For every payment, save a PDF or screenshot of the official exchange rate for that day and attach it to your records for that invoice. This simple act creates an ironclad, audit-proof paper trail, replacing guesswork with precision and protecting you from any future scrutiny.
Frequently Asked Questions
Do I absolutely need a W-8BEN form for a US client?

Yes. Providing this form proactively is a mark of professionalism. It certifies your non-U.S. status, allowing your client to pay you without withholding US taxes and removing a common cause of payment delays.
How do I calculate the EUR amount to declare from a USD invoice?

You declare the exact euro amount that arrives in your bank account on the day it is credited. This is the "cash receipt principle" (*le principe de l'encaissement*). Reconcile your records using the official ECB exchange rate for that specific day.
What is the best way to receive USD payments in France?

Use a multi-currency account to provide your client with local US ACH banking details. This makes paying you as simple and cheap as a domestic transfer for them, removing the friction of international wires.
Can I invoice a US client in dollars?
Yes, and you should. Invoicing in your client's currency is a professional courtesy. However, all your official accounting and social security declarations in France must be recorded in euros.
What if I make a mistake on a sent invoice?

You cannot simply delete it, as this breaks the mandatory sequential numbering of your invoices. The correct procedure is to issue a new, corrected invoice (*facture rectificative*) with a new number that clearly references the number of the original, now-voided invoice.
Conclusion: Your Invoice Is Your Ambassador

Your invoice is the final, critical handshake of a professional engagement. It is an ambassador for your business, speaking volumes about your reliability and expertise long after the work is complete. A clear, compliant, and thoughtfully constructed invoice does not just get you paid faster—it builds the trust that underpins a long-term, high-value partnership.

By implementing this three-pillar framework, you shift from a reactive state of compliance anxiety to a strategic position of control. You anticipate client needs with the W-8BEN, eliminate payment friction with ACH details, and mitigate risk for both parties with legally sound documentation. This operational excellence is a powerful differentiator that fosters loyalty and justifies premium rates. Clients will consistently choose the partner who makes their job easier and eliminates any shadow of financial or legal uncertainty. Your mastery of this process becomes a core part of your value proposition, cementing your reputation as an elite global professional.
